              "cropGravity": null,
              "crop": null,
              "caption": "<p>No game franchise triggers the \"just one more turn\" syndrome more than  Sid Meier's <em>Civilization</em>—in  fact, that phrase is even baked right into recent entries in the  series. Though not the first turn-based, 4X (\"eXplore\" / \"eXpand\" /  \"eXploit\" / \"eXterminate\") strategy franchise to arrive on the market,  1991's <em>Civilization</em> and its  successors quickly established themselves as the 4X games against which all others are measured, thanks to <em>Civ</em>'s hugely addictive gameplay loop and depth. </p><p>That franchise expands this week with the arrival of <a href=\"https://www.metacritic.com/game/sid-meiers-civilization-vii/\">Civilization VII</a>,  the first all-new entry in the series in nearly a decade. How does it  stack up to the previous titles in a series that has sold more than 70  million units and frequently ranks among the greatest game franchises of  all time? To find out, we have ranked every <em>Civilization</em> game  from worst to best. Those rankings are determined by each game's  Metascore, a number from 0-100 that represents the overall consensus  from the most respected game publications and professional critics. </p><p>Unfortunately, the original <em>Civilization</em> game is excluded  from our list, as its 1991 release date (which predates both Metacritic  and its one-time sister site GameRankings) means that we don't have  enough reviews available to allow us to calculate a score. That game,  developed by Sid Meier and his then-company MicroProse and loosely  inspired by a 1980 Avalon Hill board game of the same name, established  the basic gameplay template for the series—including the then-novel  technology tree and an epic scope that spans millennia—that persists to  this day, even if the implementation and complexity have changed  considerably. And Meier's name has been included as part of the official  title of nearly every release, with most subsequent games developed by  the Meier-founded studio Firaxis (now owned by Take-Two Interactive). </p><p><strong>Each title is only included once below even if was released for multiple platforms. </strong>Unless  otherwise indicated, only  the original PC version of each game was  eligible to be ranked here (though many of the titles were later ported  to consoles). The exception is games released only on consoles, in which  case the version receiving the highest quantity of critic reviews is  the one included below. <strong>In addition to the main numbered series, we have also included spin-off games</strong> (with the exceptions of 1994's <a href=\"https://www.metacritic.com/game/sid-meiers-colonization/\">Sid Meier's Colonization</a>, 1999's <a href=\"https://www.metacritic.com/game/civilization-call-to-power/\">Civilization: Call to Power</a>, and the 2011 Facebook game <em>Civilization World</em>, for which no reviews are available) <strong>but not DLC expansions</strong>.</p>",

Interactive's 2009 acquisition of the rights to adapt both Tolkien's books and Peter Jackson's films. (Previously, the rights to adapt the book and film franchises were held separately by Vivendi and EA, respectively.) Developed by Snowblind, the studio behind the excellent <em>Baldur's Gate: Dark Alliance</em>, the game borrowed its overall look from the films, though it used new voice actors and drew  elements from the books that were not in the movie trilogyâ€\"but it  didn't really share a focus with either, instead depicting a side quest taken on by a trio of playable characters: a ranger, a dwarf, and an elf, who can be played separately or all at once via online co-op. </p><p>Unlike some of the <em>LOTR</em> games already discussed, <em>North</em> was aimed at older audiences, but that maturity didn't necessarily translate to a better game. Some critics liked the co-op gameplay, but many complained about the poor AI and lack of originality, and quite a few reviewers felt that <em>North</em> wore out its welcome after just a few hours.</p><p>â€œLord of the Rings: War in the North is fine for a few hours of jolly good fun, but once you realise that the game has played its hand early and really has nothing else to offer then the lustre is soon lost. Poor AI, boring combat and pointless quests all add up to a disappointing game that would be better off dropped into the fires of Mordor.â€ â€\"Xbox Achievements</p>",

            {
              "id": "27438e90-ff43-4391-8879-8c3a5f73070f",
              "quote": "As a game, it is mediocre. The only exceptional aspects are the graphics and attention to detail. The story takes forever to gain momentum (10 to 15 hours), but once it does, it is a great story. Getting to that point was a serious slog, but I stuck with it because of how overrated this game is. This game is carried by its story, which is very unusual for gaming in general. The only game I can think of with a better story is the first BioShock (which was immediately captivating). Now as for the actual gameplay... that is where this game is very mediocre. The shooting is very simplistic, with a simple slow-motion (dead eye) mechanic being about the only thing to differentiate it from something like Uncharted or Tomb Raider. And there are some deeply frustrating game mechanics. For example, if you ride your horse past another NPC (on their horse or otherwise), that person will get offended that you just ran by them (and simply yell at you). But it happens even if you're like a foot away from them and don't touch them! It is seriously annoying, like the radius or hitboxes for an offense are way too big. Also, there is quite a bit of management tedium, such as: you need to brush your horse, you need to eat, you need to feed your horse, you need to drink tonics to restore health/stamina/dead eye, you need to trim your beard (it grows over time), you need to keep your horse out of camp to avoid offending others because it poops (the list goes on)... AND it is extremely difficult/finnicky to simply set up camp when you rest so that you can gain access to fast travel. 95% of the time it will say something like \"you cannot set up camp while there is activity nearby\", but you have no clue what supposed activity the game is detecting. Very annoying \"gameplay\" systems of tedium. Most of that crap is absent from the original Red Dead Redemption, which has superior gameplay, but an inferior story. I also think Days Gone has much better gameplay (but also an inferior story). Playing Red Dead Redemption 2 is more like reading a 50 hour book or TV series. It has a very slow start, annoying gameplay systems, and you'll spend about half of your time simply riding a horse and enjoying the scenery, but the story is what might make it worth enduring if you're in the right mood to just relax and take in the atmosphere of a very beautiful and detailed world.",

Though not the first turn-based, 4X (\"eXplore\" / \"eXpand\" /  \"eXploit\" / \"eXterminate\") strategy franchise to arrive on the market,  1991's <em>Civilization</em> and its  successors quickly established themselves as the 4X games against which all others are measured, thanks to <em>Civ</em>'s hugely addictive gameplay loop and depth. </p><p>That franchise expands this week with the arrival of <a href=\"https://www.metacritic.com/game/sid-meiers-civilization-vii/\">Civilization VII</a>,  the first all-new entry in the series in nearly a decade. Those rankings are determined by each game's  Metascore, a number from 0-100 that represents the overall consensus  from the most respected game publications and professional critics. </p><p>Unfortunately, the original <em>Civilization</em> game is excluded  from our list, as its 1991 release date (which predates both Metacritic  and its one-time sister site GameRankings) means that we don't have  enough reviews available to allow us to calculate a score.               "id": "27438e90-ff43-4391-8879-8c3a5f73070f",
              "quote": "As a game, it is mediocre. The only exceptional aspects are the graphics and attention to detail. The story takes forever to gain momentum (10 to 15 hours), but once it does, it is a great story. Getting to that point was a serious slog, but I stuck with it because of how overrated this game is. This game is carried by its story, which is very unusual for gaming in general. The only game I can think of with a better story is the first BioShock (which was immediately captivating). Now as for the actual gameplay... that is where this game is very mediocre. The shooting is very simplistic, with a simple slow-motion (dead eye) mechanic being about the only thing to differentiate it from something like Uncharted or Tomb Raider. And there are some deeply frustrating game mechanics. For example, if you ride your horse past another NPC (on their horse or otherwise), that person will get offended that you just ran by them (and simply yell at you). But it happens even if you're like a foot away from them and don't touch them! It is seriously annoying, like the radius or hitboxes for an offense are way too big. Also, there is quite a bit of management tedium, such as: you need to brush your horse, you need to eat, you need to feed your horse, you need to drink tonics to restore health/stamina/dead eye, you need to trim your beard (it grows over time), you need to keep your horse out of camp to avoid offending others because it poops (the list goes on)... AND it is extremely difficult/finnicky to simply set up camp when you rest so that you can gain access to fast travel. 95% of the time it will say something like \"you cannot set up camp while there is activity nearby\", but you have no clue what supposed activity the game is detecting. Very annoying \"gameplay\" systems of tedium. Most of that crap is absent from the original Red Dead Redemption, which has superior gameplay, but an inferior story. I also think Days Gone has much better gameplay (but also an inferior story). Playing Red Dead Redemption 2 is more like reading a 50 hour book or TV series. It has a very slow start, annoying gameplay systems, and you'll spend about half of your time simply riding a horse and enjoying the scenery, but the story is what might make it worth enduring if you're in the right mood to just relax and take in the atmosphere of a very beautiful and detailed world.",

Originally made for Polish television, Dekalog focuses on the residents of a housing complex in late-Communist Poland, whose lives become subtly intertwined as they face emotional dilemmas that are at once deeply personal and universally human. Its ten hour-long films, drawing from the Ten Commandments for thematic inspiration and an overarching structure, grapple deftly with complex moral and existential questions concerning life, death, love, hate, truth, and the passage of time. Starring Ozu regulars Chishu Ryu and Setsuko Hara, the film reprises one of the director’s favorite themes—that of generational conflict—in a way that is quintessentially Japanese and yet so universal in its appeal that it continues to resonate as one of cinema’s greatest masterpieces.               "quote": "¡ LA PRIMERA GRAN PELÍCULA DE LA HISTORIA !\nQuiero dividir esta reseña en dos partes. La primera es el apartado técnico, que es sobresaliente, rozando la excelencia. Al ser cuatro historias, hay muchos más escenarios, y todos y cada uno de ellos presentan una calidad de detalle casi enfermizo; incluso ahora hay escenas que me dejan con la boca abierta. Por supuesto, no está exenta de errores pequeños, casi imperceptibles, pero me gustaría nombrarlos. En los primeros planos, se usa un tipo de desenfoque que hace que se pierda calidad en algunas escenas, ya que al aplicarlo es necesaria una mayor iluminación, algo que se les pasa por alto en ciertas tomas. No es nada problemático, ni tampoco arruina las escenas.Como segundo apartado está la narrativa, y aquí tengo varias opiniones encontradas. Al ser cuatro historias, temía que no se profundizara en ninguna y que la narrativa, al final, se diluyera, y efectivamente así fue. El problema radica principalmente en dos aspectos. Primero, las historias no son lineales, sino que van intercalando escenas, lo que provoca que, cuando empiezas a engancharte con la historia, cambian, y ese efecto de inmersión se desvanece. El segundo punto es que, al ser tantas historias, algunas tienen más protagonismo que otras, lo que me hace preguntarme si no habría sido mejor utilizar tres o dos historias y desarrollarlas **** estas razones, no creo que sea la mejor película de la historia, como afirma cierto sector de la crítica. Creo que se dejaron apantallar con el gran despliegue técnico de la película, lo cual es bastante razonable. Sin embargo, si profundizas y no te quedas en la superficie, empiezas a notar las costuras. Da la impresión de que el autor de la película lo único que quería era demostrar que puede hacerla y que tiene los recursos para ello. La frase “mientras más grande, mejor” le queda perfecta, pues no parece haberse molestado demasiado en pensar si era necesario invertir tantos recursos solo para probar que **** no se malinterpreten mis palabras: para mí —y lo digo de forma categórica—, es la primera gran película de su tipo. Sin embargo, tengo la impresión de que podría haber sido aún mejor. Bastante recomendable.",

              "quote": "¡ LA PRIMERA GRAN PELÍCULA DE LA HISTORIA !\nQuiero dividir esta reseña en dos partes. La primera es el apartado técnico, que es sobresaliente, rozando la excelencia. Al ser cuatro historias, hay muchos más escenarios, y todos y cada uno de ellos presentan una calidad de detalle casi enfermizo; incluso ahora hay escenas que me dejan con la boca abierta. Por supuesto, no está exenta de errores pequeños, casi imperceptibles, pero me gustaría nombrarlos. En los primeros planos, se usa un tipo de desenfoque que hace que se pierda calidad en algunas escenas, ya que al aplicarlo es necesaria una mayor iluminación, algo que se les pasa por alto en ciertas tomas. No es nada problemático, ni tampoco arruina las escenas.Como segundo apartado está la narrativa, y aquí tengo varias opiniones encontradas. Al ser cuatro historias, temía que no se profundizara en ninguna y que la narrativa, al final, se diluyera, y efectivamente así fue. El problema radica principalmente en dos aspectos. Primero, las historias no son lineales, sino que van intercalando escenas, lo que provoca que, cuando empiezas a engancharte con la historia, cambian, y ese efecto de inmersión se desvanece. El segundo punto es que, al ser tantas historias, algunas tienen más protagonismo que otras, lo que me hace preguntarme si no habría sido mejor utilizar tres o dos historias y desarrollarlas **** estas razones, no creo que sea la mejor película de la historia, como afirma cierto sector de la crítica. Creo que se dejaron apantallar con el gran despliegue técnico de la película, lo cual es bastante razonable. Sin embargo, si profundizas y no te quedas en la superficie, empiezas a notar las costuras. Da la impresión de que el autor de la película lo único que quería era demostrar que puede hacerla y que tiene los recursos para ello. La frase “mientras más grande, mejor” le queda perfecta, pues no parece haberse molestado demasiado en pensar si era necesario invertir tantos recursos solo para probar que **** no se malinterpreten mis palabras: para mí —y lo digo de forma categórica—, es la primera gran película de su tipo. Sin embargo, tengo la impresión de que podría haber sido aún mejor. Bastante recomendable.",

              "id": "4a418ee6-6052-4dfb-9970-5bf12571f004",
              "quote": "¡ LA PRIMERA GRAN PELÍCULA DE LA HISTORIA !\nQuiero dividir esta reseña en dos partes. La primera es el apartado técnico, que es sobresaliente, rozando la excelencia. Al ser cuatro historias, hay muchos más escenarios, y todos y cada uno de ellos presentan una calidad de detalle casi enfermizo; incluso ahora hay escenas que me dejan con la boca abierta. Por supuesto, no está exenta de errores pequeños, casi imperceptibles, pero me gustaría nombrarlos. En los primeros planos, se usa un tipo de desenfoque que hace que se pierda calidad en algunas escenas, ya que al aplicarlo es necesaria una mayor iluminación, algo que se les pasa por alto en ciertas tomas. No es nada problemático, ni tampoco arruina las escenas.Como segundo apartado está la narrativa, y aquí tengo varias opiniones encontradas. Al ser cuatro historias, temía que no se profundizara en ninguna y que la narrativa, al final, se diluyera, y efectivamente así fue. El problema radica principalmente en dos aspectos. Primero, las historias no son lineales, sino que van intercalando escenas, lo que provoca que, cuando empiezas a engancharte con la historia, cambian, y ese efecto de inmersión se desvanece. El segundo punto es que, al ser tantas historias, algunas tienen más protagonismo que otras, lo que me hace preguntarme si no habría sido mejor utilizar tres o dos historias y desarrollarlas **** estas razones, no creo que sea la mejor película de la historia, como afirma cierto sector de la crítica. Creo que se dejaron apantallar con el gran despliegue técnico de la película, lo cual es bastante razonable. Sin embargo, si profundizas y no te quedas en la superficie, empiezas a notar las costuras. Da la impresión de que el autor de la película lo único que quería era demostrar que puede hacerla y que tiene los recursos para ello. La frase “mientras más grande, mejor” le queda perfecta, pues no parece haberse molestado demasiado en pensar si era necesario invertir tantos recursos solo para probar que **** no se malinterpreten mis palabras: para mí —y lo digo de forma categórica—, es la primera gran película de su tipo.             {
              "id": "f8429c85-e8df-488c-9563-9a30dff58c64",
              "quote": "David Attenborough, as has been said many times, is wholly deserving of being called a national treasure, although it is a term he happens to not like. He has done so many treasures and even his lesser output of a long and consistently impressive career is still good.\n\nAbsolutely adore the first 'The Blue Planet', one of my favourites of his, so was psyched to hear that there was a second 'Blue Planet' series. Luckily, 'Blue Planet II' turned out to be every bit as amazing, easily a highlight of 2017 television (a strong contender for the best BBC produced one and the best nature documentary series) and one of not many 2017 dramas/series that had me looking forward to see the entire lot. This is saying a lot, seeing as apart from the odd gem 2017 has not seen me watching new television by habit, often find myself seeing re-runs or films.\n\nOK, so 'Blue Planet II' may not be as ground-breaking as 'The Blue Planet' and not everything is new here. This doesn't matter, because 'Blue Planet II' is just as beautiful to watch, non-stop transfixing, educational, inspirational and emotionally complex.\n\nVisually, 'Blue Planet II' was, as it was with 'The Blue Planet' and the best of Attenborough's work, a wonder. It has gorgeous scenery and rich colours, while the animals and marine life are captured in all their glory. Standing out even more is the photography, the underwater sequences are just as stunning as 'The Blue Planet' (unequalled when it comes to underwater sequences).\n\nWhile not with the involvement of George Fenton, the music here soars, rouses just as much and touches the soul just as much, definitely worthy of cinematic quality. It not only complements the visuals but enhances them to a greater level.\n\nReally can't fault the narrative aspects in 'Blue Planet II' either. There are things already known to me, still delivered with a lot of freshness, but there was a lot that was quite an education and after watching the full series it honestly felt like the series taught me a lot about the mysteries of the ocean and the marine life that inhabit it. One episode explores the consequences of environmental waste and, while it may seem out of place for some, it was very much relevant and important and well-handled.\n\nAttenborough's narration helps quite significantly too, he clearly knows his stuff and knows what to say and how to say it. He delivers it with his usual richness, soft-spoken enthusiasm and sincerity, never talking down to the viewer and keeping them riveted and wanting to know more.\n\n'Blue Planet II' has a lot of effective scenes. It's not easy picking a standout, because there is a lot of emotional complexity here. There are shockingly gut-wrenching moments, especially the whale carcass (a strong contender for the most shocking scene of 2017). There are emotional moments, such as the mother with the dead calf. There are tense moments like the octopus and the shark. There are awe-inspiring moments like with the dolphins. There are also moments of great surprise, like in the first episode where one expects a bird to fall prey to an underwater predator but instead something that one cannot believe was able to be filmed let alone found happens.\n\nNothing episodic or repetitive here. Instead, it feels like its own individual story with real, complex emotions and conflicts. One roots for the animals, whether prey or predator. The behind the scenes footage \"The Deep Blue\" brings honesty and humanity, what the crew go through and how they work against sometimes volatile conditions makes the viewer feel admiration for them.\n\nIn short, utterly amazing and calling 'Blue Planet II' a must watch does it no justice. 10/10 Bethany Cox",

    {
      "data": {
        "id": "1000358361",
        "totalResults": 7,
        "item": {
          "default": [
            {
              "id": "e75af6c2-8e36-4b2d-ac57-3f5bd7b62048",
              "quote": "1.Vimper dairy  \nUnderCORNDARE\n\n2.ROCK VANDEM \n\n3.tompo vs shozniger\n\nRambo Silverstone \n",
              "score": 10,
              "thumbsUp": null,
              "thumbsDown": null,
              "date": "2026-03-21",
              "author": "SKITLANORSE",
              "version": 1774066970357,
              "spoiler": true,
              "reviewedProduct": {
                "id": 1000358361,
                "type": "shows",
                "title": "Planet Earth: Blue Planet II",
                "url": "/tv/planet-earth-blue-planet-ii/",
                "tvTaxonomy": {
                  "show": {
                    "id": 1000358361,
                    "name": "Planet Earth: Blue Planet II"
                  }
                }
              }
            },
            {
              "id": "f8429c85-e8df-488c-9563-9a30dff58c64",
              "quote": "David Attenborough, as has been said many times, is wholly deserving of being called a national treasure, although it is a term he happens to not like. He has done so many treasures and even his lesser output of a long and consistently impressive career is still good.\n\nAbsolutely adore the first 'The Blue Planet', one of my favourites of his, so was psyched to hear that there was a second 'Blue Planet' series. Luckily, 'Blue Planet II' turned out to be every bit as amazing, easily a highlight of 2017 television (a strong contender for the best BBC produced one and the best nature documentary series) and one of not many 2017 dramas/series that had me looking forward to see the entire lot. This is saying a lot, seeing as apart from the odd gem 2017 has not seen me watching new television by habit, often find myself seeing re-runs or films.\n\nOK, so 'Blue Planet II' may not be as ground-breaking as 'The Blue Planet' and not everything is new here. This doesn't matter, because 'Blue Planet II' is just as beautiful to watch, non-stop transfixing, educational, inspirational and emotionally complex.\n\nVisually, 'Blue Planet II' was, as it was with 'The Blue Planet' and the best of Attenborough's work, a wonder. It has gorgeous scenery and rich colours, while the animals and marine life are captured in all their glory. Standing out even more is the photography, the underwater sequences are just as stunning as 'The Blue Planet' (unequalled when it comes to underwater sequences).\n\nWhile not with the involvement of George Fenton, the music here soars, rouses just as much and touches the soul just as much, definitely worthy of cinematic quality. It not only complements the visuals but enhances them to a greater level.\n\nReally can't fault the narrative aspects in 'Blue Planet II' either. There are things already known to me, still delivered with a lot of freshness, but there was a lot that was quite an education and after watching the full series it honestly felt like the series taught me a lot about the mysteries of the ocean and the marine life that inhabit it. One episode explores the consequences of environmental waste and, while it may seem out of place for some, it was very much relevant and important and well-handled.\n\nAttenborough's narration helps quite significantly too, he clearly knows his stuff and knows what to say and how to say it. He delivers it with his usual richness, soft-spoken enthusiasm and sincerity, never talking down to the viewer and keeping them riveted and wanting to know more.\n\n'Blue Planet II' has a lot of effective scenes. It's not easy picking a standout, because there is a lot of emotional complexity here. There are shockingly gut-wrenching moments, especially the whale carcass (a strong contender for the most shocking scene of 2017). There are emotional moments, such as the mother with the dead calf. There are tense moments like the octopus and the shark. There are awe-inspiring moments like with the dolphins. There are also moments of great surprise, like in the first episode where one expects a bird to fall prey to an underwater predator but instead something that one cannot believe was able to be filmed let alone found happens.\n\nNothing episodic or repetitive here. Instead, it feels like its own individual story with real, complex emotions and conflicts. One roots for the animals, whether prey or predator. The behind the scenes footage \"The Deep Blue\" brings honesty and humanity, what the crew go through and how they work against sometimes volatile conditions makes the viewer feel admiration for them.\n\nIn short, utterly amazing and calling 'Blue Planet II' a must watch does it no justice. 10/10 Bethany Cox",

            {
              "id": "f8429c85-e8df-488c-9563-9a30dff58c64",
              "quote": "David Attenborough, as has been said many times, is wholly deserving of being called a national treasure, although it is a term he happens to not like. He has done so many treasures and even his lesser output of a long and consistently impressive career is still good.\n\nAbsolutely adore the first 'The Blue Planet', one of my favourites of his, so was psyched to hear that there was a second 'Blue Planet' series. Luckily, 'Blue Planet II' turned out to be every bit as amazing, easily a highlight of 2017 television (a strong contender for the best BBC produced one and the best nature documentary series) and one of not many 2017 dramas/series that had me looking forward to see the entire lot. This is saying a lot, seeing as apart from the odd gem 2017 has not seen me watching new television by habit, often find myself seeing re-runs or films.\n\nOK, so 'Blue Planet II' may not be as ground-breaking as 'The Blue Planet' and not everything is new here. This doesn't matter, because 'Blue Planet II' is just as beautiful to watch, non-stop transfixing, educational, inspirational and emotionally complex.\n\nVisually, 'Blue Planet II' was, as it was with 'The Blue Planet' and the best of Attenborough's work, a wonder. It has gorgeous scenery and rich colours, while the animals and marine life are captured in all their glory. Standing out even more is the photography, the underwater sequences are just as stunning as 'The Blue Planet' (unequalled when it comes to underwater sequences).\n\nWhile not with the involvement of George Fenton, the music here soars, rouses just as much and touches the soul just as much, definitely worthy of cinematic quality. It not only complements the visuals but enhances them to a greater level.\n\nReally can't fault the narrative aspects in 'Blue Planet II' either. There are things already known to me, still delivered with a lot of freshness, but there was a lot that was quite an education and after watching the full series it honestly felt like the series taught me a lot about the mysteries of the ocean and the marine life that inhabit it. One episode explores the consequences of environmental waste and, while it may seem out of place for some, it was very much relevant and important and well-handled.\n\nAttenborough's narration helps quite significantly too, he clearly knows his stuff and knows what to say and how to say it. He delivers it with his usual richness, soft-spoken enthusiasm and sincerity, never talking down to the viewer and keeping them riveted and wanting to know more.\n\n'Blue Planet II' has a lot of effective scenes. It's not easy picking a standout, because there is a lot of emotional complexity here. There are shockingly gut-wrenching moments, especially the whale carcass (a strong contender for the most shocking scene of 2017). There are emotional moments, such as the mother with the dead calf. There are tense moments like the octopus and the shark. There are awe-inspiring moments like with the dolphins. There are also moments of great surprise, like in the first episode where one expects a bird to fall prey to an underwater predator but instead something that one cannot believe was able to be filmed let alone found happens.\n\nNothing episodic or repetitive here. Instead, it feels like its own individual story with real, complex emotions and conflicts. One roots for the animals, whether prey or predator. The behind the scenes footage \"The Deep Blue\" brings honesty and humanity, what the crew go through and how they work against sometimes volatile conditions makes the viewer feel admiration for them.\n\nIn short, utterly amazing and calling 'Blue Planet II' a must watch does it no justice. 10/10 Bethany Cox",
